questions.statement: Three resistors each of resistance 6 &Omega; are to be connected so that the equivalent resistance is&nbsp;4 &Omega;. Which connection is CORRECT?
options: Two in parallel and then in series with third /
Two in series and then in parallel with third&nbsp; /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Two in series and then in parallel with third.

Key Points

  • To achieve an equivalent resistance of 4 Ω using three resistors, each of 6 Ω, the configuration must involve a combination of series and parallel connections.
  • In the correct configuration, two resistors are connected in series, and their combined resistance is then connected in parallel with the third resistor. This satisfies the equivalent resistance of 4 Ω.
  • When two resistors of 6 Ω each are connected in series, the total resistance becomes:
    R_series = 6 + 6 = 12 Ω.
  • This 12 Ω series combination is then connected in parallel with the third resistor (6 Ω). Using the parallel resistance formula:
    1/R_eq = 1/R1 + 1/R2, we get:
    1/R_eq = 1/12 + 1/6 = (1 + 2)/12 = 3/12 = 1/4, so R_eq = 4 Ω.
  • Thus, the correct configuration to achieve 4 Ω is: two resistors in series, connected in parallel with the third.

Additional Information

  • Resistors in Series:
    • When resistors are connected end-to-end, they are said to be in series.
    • The total resistance is the sum of individual resistances: R_total = R1 + R2 + R3 + ....
    • In a series connection, the current remains the same across all resistors, but the voltage divides.
  • Resistors in Parallel:
    • When resistors are connected such that their ends meet at common points, they are in parallel.
    • The reciprocal of the total resistance is the sum of the reciprocals of individual resistances: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3 + ....
    • In a parallel connection, the voltage remains the same across all resistors, but the current divides.
  • Combination of Series and Parallel:
    • In practical circuits, resistors are often arranged in combinations of series and parallel to achieve a desired equivalent resistance.
    • In such configurations, the equivalent resistance is calculated by first simplifying the series or parallel sections step by step.
  • Practical Applications:
    • Such resistor configurations are used in electrical circuits for load balancing, voltage division, and current control.
    • Examples include voltage regulators, electronic devices, and power distribution systems.

Important Points

  • Option 1 (All in Parallel):
    • If all three 6 Ω resistors are connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance would be:
      1/R_eq = 1/6 + 1/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2, so R_eq = 2 Ω.
    • This does not match the required 4 Ω, so this option is incorrect.
  • Option 2 (Two in Parallel and in Series with Third):
    • If two resistors are connected in parallel, their equivalent resistance is:
      1/R_parallel = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3, so R_parallel = 3 Ω.
    • This 3 Ω combination in series with the third resistor gives:
      R_eq = 3 + 6 = 9 Ω, which does not match the required 4 Ω.
    • Hence, this option is incorrect.
  • Option 3 (Two in Series and in Parallel with Third):
    • This configuration yields an equivalent resistance of 4 Ω, as calculated above.
    • This matches the given requirement and is the correct option.
  • Option 4 (All in Series):
    • If all three resistors are connected in series, the total resistance is:
      R_eq = 6 + 6 + 6 = 18 Ω.
    • This does not match the required 4 Ω, so this option is incorrect.

questions.statement: Two wires, A and B, of the same material, have the same length, but the cross-sectional area of wire A is twice that of wire B. The ratio of their resistances \( R_A : R_B \) is:
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is 1 : 2.

Key Points

  • Resistance and its formula: The resistance (R) of a wire is given by the formula: R = ρ × (L / A), where:
    • ρ (rho): Resistivity of the material (same for both wires, as they are of the same material).
    • L: Length of the wire (same for both wires).
    • A: Cross-sectional area of the wire.
  • Given condition: Wire A has twice the cross-sectional area of wire B. Let the cross-sectional area of wire B be A, then the cross-sectional area of wire A will be 2A.
  • Relation between resistances: Since L and ρ are the same for both wires, the resistance ratio depends only on their cross-sectional areas. Using the formula R ∝ 1/A:
    • Resistance of wire A, R_A = ρ × (L / 2A).
    • Resistance of wire B, R_B = ρ × (L / A).
    • Taking the ratio, R_A : R_B = (L / 2A) : (L / A) = 1 : 2.
  • Final result: The ratio of their resistances is 1 : 2.

Additional Information

  • Factors affecting resistance: The resistance of a wire depends on:
    • Length (L): Resistance increases with an increase in length.
    • Cross-sectional area (A): Resistance decreases with an increase in area.
    • Material: The resistivity (ρ) of the material determines how much resistance it offers.
    • Temperature: For most materials, resistance increases with an increase in temperature.
  • Applications of resistance:
    • Resistors are used in electrical circuits to control the flow of current.
    • High-resistance materials are used as heating elements in devices like toasters and electric heaters.
  • Understanding resistivity:
    • Resistivity (ρ) is a material property that quantifies how strongly a material opposes the flow of electric current.
    • It is measured in ohm-meters (Ω·m).
    • Materials with low resistivity (like copper and silver) are good conductors, while materials with high resistivity (like rubber and glass) are insulators.
  • Practical implications:
    • Thicker wires (larger cross-sectional area) have lower resistance and are used in high-current applications.
    • Longer wires have higher resistance, so they are avoided in designs where minimal energy loss is required.

questions.statement: An electric bulb is rated <span class="math-tex">\( 110 \)</span> Watt &ndash; <span class="math-tex">\( 220 \)</span> Volt. The current drawn by the bulb is:
When the current in a circuit is doubled, keeping resistance constant, the power dissipated in the resistor becomes:
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Four times.

Key Points

  • The power dissipated in a resistor is calculated using the formula P = I²R, where P is the power, I is the current, and R is the resistance.
  • If the current in the circuit is doubled, the new current becomes 2I. Substituting this into the formula: P = (2I)²R = 4I²R.
  • This shows that the power dissipated becomes four times the original power when the current is doubled while keeping the resistance constant.
  • This relationship arises because power is proportional to the square of the current. Therefore, any change in current has a squared effect on the power.
  • Hence, the correct answer is that the power dissipated in the resistor becomes four times.

Additional Information

  • Ohm's Law:
    • Ohm's Law states that V = IR, where V is the voltage across the resistor, I is the current flowing through it, and R is the resistance.
    • It highlights the linear relationship between voltage and current for a constant resistance.
  • Power Dissipation in Resistors:
    • The power dissipated in a resistor can be expressed in three equivalent forms: P = I²R, P = V²/R, and P = IV.
    • These formulas help in analyzing circuits under different conditions, such as constant voltage or constant current.
  • Role of Resistance:
    • Resistance is a measure of how much a material opposes the flow of current. It is measured in ohms (Ω).
    • For a fixed resistance, changes in current or voltage directly affect the power dissipation.
  • Proportionality of Power:
    • Power is directly proportional to the square of the current (P ∝ I²) and inversely proportional to the resistance (P ∝ 1/R) for constant voltage.
    • This quadratic relationship explains why doubling the current quadruples the power dissipation.

Important Points

  • Current and Voltage Relationship:
    • When resistance is constant, changes in current are directly proportional to changes in voltage, as per Ohm's Law.
    • For instance, doubling the voltage across a resistor doubles the current flowing through it.
  • Real-Life Application:
    • Understanding power dissipation is crucial in designing electrical circuits to prevent overheating and ensure efficiency.
    • For example, resistors in electronic devices are rated for specific power levels to avoid damage.
  • Safety Considerations:
    • Excessive power dissipation can lead to overheating, which may damage components or cause fire hazards in electrical systems.
    • Engineers use heat sinks, cooling systems, or higher-rated components to manage power dissipation effectively.

questions.statement: If 100 J of work is required to transfer 5 C of charge between two points, the potential difference between the points is:
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is 20 V.

Key Points

  • The potential difference (V) between two points is calculated using the formula:
    V = Work Done (W) / Charge (Q).
  • In the given question, Work Done (W) = 100 J and Charge (Q) = 5 C.
  • Using the formula,
    V = 100 J / 5 C = 20 V.
  • The unit of potential difference is the volt (V), which is defined as 1 joule of work per coulomb of charge.
  • Hence, the potential difference between the two points is 20 V.

Additional Information

  • Potential Difference:
    • Potential difference is the work done to move a unit charge from one point to another in an electric field.
    • It is a scalar quantity and is measured in volts (V).
    • The formula is given as V = W/Q, where:
      • V is the potential difference in volts.
      • W is the work done in joules (J).
      • Q is the charge in coulombs (C).
  • Charge (Q):
    • Charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force in an electric field.
    • The SI unit of charge is the coulomb (C), where 1 coulomb is equivalent to the charge of approximately 6.242 × 1018 electrons.
    • Charge can be positive or negative, depending on the type of particle.
  • Work Done (W):
    • Work done in an electric field is the energy required to move a charge against the electric force.
    • The SI unit of work is the joule (J).
    • It is related to the potential difference and charge by the formula W = V × Q.
  • Volt (V):
    • The volt is the derived unit for electric potential, potential difference, and electromotive force in the International System of Units (SI).
    • 1 volt is defined as 1 joule per coulomb, i.e., 1 V = 1 J/C.
    • Named after the Italian physicist Alessandro Volta, who invented the first chemical battery.

Important Points

  • Ohm's Law: Ohm’s law relates voltage (V), current (I), and resistance (R) as V = I × R.
  • Electric Field: The electric field is the force per unit charge and is expressed as E = V/d, where d is the distance between two points.
  • Applications: Potential difference is a key concept in circuits, batteries, capacitors, and other electrical devices.
  • Practical Examples: A 1.5V AA battery has a potential difference of 1.5 volts between its terminals, enabling current to flow in a circuit.

questions.statement: Three resistors of \( 4\ \Omega \), \( 8\ \Omega \), and \( 12\ \Omega \) are connected in series across a \( 12\ \text{V} \) battery. The current in the circuit is:
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is 0.5 A.

Key Points

  • The given resistors of 4 Ω, 8 Ω, and 12 Ω are connected in series. In a series connection, the total or equivalent resistance is the sum of the individual resistances.
  • The formula to calculate the total resistance in a series circuit is:
    Rtotal = R1 + R2 + R3.
    Substituting the given values:
    Rtotal = 4 Ω + 8 Ω + 12 Ω = 24 Ω.
  • The circuit is powered by a 12 V battery. Using Ohm's Law, which is V = IR, we can calculate the current in the circuit. Rearranging the formula:
    I = V / R.
  • Substitute the values for voltage and total resistance:
    I = 12 V / 24 Ω = 0.5 A.
  • Thus, the current in the circuit is 0.5 A, which matches the given correct option.

Additional Information

  • Ohm's Law:
    • Ohm's Law states that the current (I) through a conductor between two points is directly proportional to the voltage (V) across the two points and inversely proportional to the resistance (R) of the conductor.
    • The formula is expressed as: V = IR, where V is voltage, I is current, and R is resistance.
    • This law is fundamental in understanding the behavior of electrical circuits.
  • Series Circuit:
    • In a series circuit, the current remains the same across all components, but the voltage is divided among them.
    • The total resistance in a series circuit is the sum of all individual resistances: Rtotal = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn.
  • Resistors in Series vs. Parallel:
    • In a series connection, resistors add up to increase the total resistance, while in a parallel connection, the total resistance decreases.
    • The formula for total resistance in a parallel circuit is:
      1/Rtotal = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... + 1/Rn.
  • Electric Current:
    • Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor.
    • The unit of electric current is the ampere (A), named after French physicist André-Marie Ampère.
    • 1 ampere is equivalent to 1 coulomb of charge passing through a point in 1 second.

Important Points

  • Power in an Electrical Circuit:
    • The power (P) dissipated in an electrical circuit is calculated using the formula: P = VI, where V is voltage and I is current.
    • For resistors, power can also be expressed as: P = I²R or P = V²/R.
  • Applications of Series Circuits:
    • Series circuits are commonly used in applications where the same current is required to flow through multiple components, such as decorative lights.
    • However, if one component in the series circuit fails, the entire circuit is interrupted.
  • Units of Measurement:
    • Voltage: Measured in volts (V).
    • Resistance: Measured in ohms (Ω).
    • Current: Measured in amperes (A).

questions.statement: In a circuit diagram, the symbol for a cell is represented by which of the following?
options: A circle with a cross inside /
One long and one short line parallel to each other /
Two equal lines parallel to each other /
A rectangle with a diagonal line /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is One long and one short line parallel to each other.

Key Points

  • The symbol for a cell in a circuit diagram is represented by one long and one short line parallel to each other. The long line represents the positive terminal, and the short line represents the negative terminal.
  • This standard symbol is used universally in electrical and electronic circuit diagrams to represent a single electrochemical cell.
  • The long line (+) indicates the higher electric potential, while the short line (-) indicates the lower electric potential. This helps in understanding the flow of current in the circuit.
  • A single cell symbol can also be combined to represent a battery, where multiple cells are connected in series or parallel.
  • In contrast, other options like a “circle with a cross inside,” “two equal lines parallel to each other,” or “a rectangle with a diagonal line” do not represent a cell in circuit diagrams. These symbols represent different components like bulbs, resistors, or other devices.

Additional Information

  • Electrochemical Cell:
    • An electrochemical cell is a device capable of either generating electrical energy from chemical reactions or using electrical energy to cause chemical reactions.
    • It consists of two electrodes (anode and cathode) and an electrolyte. The anode is the negative terminal, while the cathode is the positive terminal.
  • Battery Symbol:
    • A battery symbol in a circuit diagram is represented by multiple cells connected together, where the number of long and short lines indicates the number of cells in the battery.
    • Batteries are used in various devices, from small electronic gadgets to large-scale power storage systems.
  • Electric Circuit Components:
    • Bulb: Represented by a circle with a cross inside in circuit diagrams. It shows the presence of a light-emitting component in the circuit.
    • Resistor: Represented by a rectangle or zigzag line, indicating a component that resists the flow of current.
    • Switch: Denoted by a break in the line with a lever, it indicates a controlling device that can open or close the circuit.
  • Direction of Current:
    • The conventional direction of current flow is from the positive terminal (long line) to the negative terminal (short line) of the cell.
    • This aligns with the movement of positive charges, even though electrons actually flow in the opposite direction.

Important Points

  • Standards in Circuit Diagrams: The symbols used in circuit diagrams follow international conventions set by organizations like IEC (International Electrotechnical Commission) to ensure uniformity and understanding.
  • Applications of Cells: Electrochemical cells are used in batteries, portable devices, vehicles, and energy storage systems for renewable energy applications.
  • Types of Cells: Cells can be classified as primary cells (non-rechargeable) and secondary cells (rechargeable), based on their ability to be reused.
  • Real-World Example: A common example of a cell is an AA battery, widely used in household devices like remote controls and clocks.

questions.statement: An electric heater of resistance <span class="math-tex">\( 20 \Omega \)</span> operates for 10 minutes when connected to a 220 V supply. The heat produced is:
The storage battery of a car has an EMF of 32 V. If the internal resistance of the battery is 0.8 &Omega;, what is the maximum current that can be drawn from the battery?
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is 40 A.

Key Points

  • The formula to calculate the maximum current (I) that can be drawn from the battery is: I = EMF / Internal Resistance.
  • Given, EMF = 32 V and internal resistance = 0.8 Ω.
  • Substitute the values: I = 32 / 0.8 = 40 A.
  • Maximum current refers to the current that can flow when the battery is short-circuited, i.e., when the external resistance is zero.
  • It is important to note that drawing the maximum current continuously can damage the battery due to excessive heat generation.

Additional Information

  • Option 2: 55 A
    • This value is incorrect because it does not align with the calculation using the formula I = EMF / Internal Resistance.
    • If this value were correct, it would imply an unrealistically low internal resistance for a 32 V battery.
  • Option 3: 30 A
    • This value is also incorrect as it underestimates the maximum current the battery can supply.
    • Such a value could arise from incorrect substitution or misinterpretation of the formula.
  • Option 4: 70 A
    • This value overestimates the maximum current and is not consistent with the given parameters of the problem.
    • To reach this value, the internal resistance would need to be much lower than 0.8 Ω, which is not the case.
  • Concept of EMF and Internal Resistance:
    • The electromotive force (EMF) is the total voltage provided by the battery when no current is drawn.
    • Internal resistance is the resistance within the battery that opposes the flow of current.
    • The maximum current can be calculated using Ohm's Law and is limited by the internal resistance of the battery.
